issues.title: Move supported autorization details types into a separate endpoint
issues.author: runegri
issues.body:
An AS may support a large number of structures, this would make the metadata document very large. Even though this document normally is cached on the web server, there is the issue of network traffic and the load of parsing the metadata.

I suggest that instead of including the structures in the metadata document we reference a separate endpoint that serves the supported types. This way the metadata document will only indicate that the feature is supported (which is useful for discovery) by supplying the url of the endpoint. 

The content served from the new endpoint would be similar to the current specification.

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body: I see your point and agree. Let me discuss this further and consider positively

issues.title: We need a better explanation of the use cases for the insufficient_authorization_details signal
issues.author: runegri
issues.body:
I am having trouble understanding the use case for the insufficient_authorization_details signal where the Resource Server indicates the authorization details structure that sould be included in the request. 

If the RS knows what data should be included in the request I don't see the need for this feature. And in the other case, where the RS does not know the data, then I don't see how this feature can be used. So there is something I don't understand here :-)

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body:
Thanks for the feedback,
Let me try and explain. Imagine client attempts a state changing RS call (PUT / POST etc + body).
RS requires end-user's approval BUT the RAR object is not simply 1:1 the HTTP request body.
Maybe it's structured differently, maybe it requires information available to RS but not to client, such as a calculated risk_score, or risk_ticket_id with which AS can retrieve additional instructions as to required challenges.

So this option is a step-up mechanism, with which client doesn't need to learn how to construct a RAR object but gets from RS a ready made one, which may include ephemeral data as described here.

comments.author: runegri
comments.body:
But would the RS be able to supply that information? I can imagine that th RS could request the "step-up" and even indicate that it needs the information supplied in a given RAR type, but I am having trouble seeing how the RS could know how to build the complete RAR object. 

In my ecosystem (The Norwegian health sector) we use several different RAR structures to supply extended information about the user context that the RS needs. Without that information all the RS can do is to reject the request and possibly specify the information that is missing.

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body:
We have such use-cases in banking (payment approvals, trading approvals: Stocks, Crypto, FX) where the RS enforces the RAR-containing access token and so knows what the valid RAR object should look like for a specific request and can provide it in a WWW-Authenticate header.

I assume there will be 2 emerging patters, both valid:
- Expose metadata so client can figure out how to craft RAR objects
- AS provides ready to use RAR objects

comments.author: runegri
comments.body:
This is interesting! I would love to hear more about the use cases :-)

I imagine we would want to describe a third case where the AS returns the type of RAR object it expects without supplying the actual object. Then it would be up to the client to craft the correct structure based on the templates found in the metadata.

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body:
So I'm leaning towards RS always failing with WWW-Authenticate + error="insufficient_authorization_details".
And OPTIONALLY it can also provide in the body the required authorization_details object.

In the metadata discovery path, resource metadata endpoint shall name the rar_types expected by the resource, but not provide the schema. Client will get that from the new `authorization_details_types_metadata_endpoint`.

Standby for next version of the spec, work in progress

issues.title: Security considerations for client manipulation of authorization details
issues.author: max-stytch
issues.body:
In `3.2. Client obtains authorization details object from resource server's error response` - the client receives an authorization details object from the resource server. The client then replays that authorization details object in a RAR OAuth request.

The authorization details object is cleartext - it is not signed or encrypted. The client can manipulate the authorization details object before replaying it to the authorization server. This means that no parameter within the RAR request can be trusted.

Taking the example from `A.3.2. Resource server signals insufficient_authorization_details with actionable RAR object`:
```json
{
    "authorization_details": [{
      "type": "payment_initiation",
      "locations": [
          "https://example.com/payments"
      ],
      "instructedAmount": {
          "currency": "EUR",
          "amount": "123.50"
      },
      "creditorName": "Merchant A",
      "creditorAccount": {
          "bic": "ABCIDEFFXXX",
          "iban": "DE02100100109307118603"
      },
      "interactionId": "f81d4fae-7dec-11d0-a765-00a0c91e6bf6",
      "riskProfile": "B-71"
}]
}
```

The client can change the fields to (possibly):
- Request a token with permissions to move a larger amount of money than originally requested
- Spoof the creditor name (e.g. from `Evil Merchant` to `Merchant A`)
- Substitute new values for `interactionId` and `riskProfile`

[9396](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9396#name-security-considerations) defines security considerations for manipulation of RAR payloads by the user-agent by using PAR and JAR:
> The authorization_details parameter is sent through the user agent in case of an OAuth authorization request, which makes them vulnerable to modifications by the user. If the integrity of the authorization_details is a concern, clients MUST protect authorization_details against tampering and swapping. 

However, PAR and JAR are both initiated by the client, and we don't have a mechanism for a resource server to defend against tampering of an `authorization_details` object. Do we need one?

Does this mean that the challenge response can never be used in cases where the integrity of the `authorization_details` from the RS is a concern? Can fields like `riskProfile` ever be safely embedded?

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body:
Thanks @max-stytch,
A very valid point which was mentioned also by RFC9396 co-author Justin Richer when the draft was presented 16.03 at IETF 125. I think several solution patterns are possible.

First prerequisite would be that RS must have a way to validate access tokens of incoming requests have been issued by OAuth flows that contained required RAR objects. This depends on how RAR is represented in access tokens (or introspection response), both are out of scope of RFC 9396.

I assume AS has got to either include the RAR objects, or array of their hashes in the access token (or introspection response).
RS can then reconstruct expected RAR from request body, [hashes and] checks against AT / introspection response, detecting unauthorized modification of requested RAR object or request body.

Question: If adversary modifies **both** RAR and request body so they match, provided end-user has given permission, validation will succeed. Should this case be mitigated?

Access token / introspection response representation of RAR objects is new oauth work that needs to be discussed in the IETF oauth WG.

We could consider additional authorization server mechanisms to validate integrity of requested RAR objects when they are provided by resource server:
1. Resource server can provide a detached JWS signature (RFC 7515 appendix F) authorization server part of RAR object or authorization server an auxiliary object. authorization server can use resource= oauth request parameter to pull resource server's jwks_uri and validate RAR object integrity.
2. New pushed RAR objects endpoint, to be called by resource server, enabling direct resource server to authorization server communication removing client authorization server adveresource serverary channel. authorization server stores RAR alongside resource and returns rar_uri, which resource server provides to client to be included in oauth request, alongside resource parameter for validation.

comments.author: max-stytch
comments.body:
> First prerequisite would be that RS must have a way to validate access tokens of incoming requests have been issued by OAuth flows that contained required RAR objects. This depends on how RAR is represented in access tokens (or introspection response), both are out of scope of RFC 9396.

[9396 section 9](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9396.html#name-resource-servers) defines RAR representation for both access tokens and introspection responses already. Do we need something additional here?

> New pushed RAR objects endpoint, to be called by resource server, enabling direct resource server to authorization server communication

I am a big fan of a pushed RAR object endpoint - I think it does a good job of ensuring that RAR objects of larger sizes can be passed around, and it eliminates the need to return the RAR object in the response body on challenge responses. This would free up the response body to continue to hold application-specific error messages and error formats.

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body:
> [9396 section 9](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9396.html#name-resource-servers) defines RAR representation for both access tokens and introspection responses already. Do we need something additional here?
> 
> >
We've encountered size problems with multiple RAR objects bloating access tokens so much that ingress header limits were reached.
We therefore have opted for hashing RAR objects in JWT access tokens.
The pattern of RAR introspection also solves size problems, don't know if it can be combined with JWT access tokens.

> I am a big fan of a pushed RAR object endpoint - I think it does a good job of ensuring that RAR objects of larger sizes can be passed around, and it eliminates the need to return the RAR object in the response body on challenge responses. This would free up the response body to continue to hold application-specific error messages and error formats.
> 
> >
Justin says:
GNAP solved this same kind of problem by allowing the RS to register a resource set (as a RAR like object) and get back a reference (which is the GNAP equivalent of a scope string — but in this case it’s likely a random value and not something a developer would pull from documentation). https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9767.html#name-registering-a-resource-set.
In GNAP, this reference can be sent by the client alongside whatever client-provided access requests that you want. So an attacker can always ALSO ask for more if the AS allows it.

Now this is GNAP not OAuth so he's going to comment here if such a pattern may be used as is with OAuth.

comments.author: jricher
comments.body:
Yes, this registration pattern could be used in OAuth by defining a resource registration endpoint, which exactly is what UMA did many years ago now as well. The response of this registration can be returned to the client as a scope. The client doesn't need to know or care what that scope means, and it can ask for additional scopes or RAR objects as well.

I would recommend starting with the gnap protocol definition and adapting it slightly to fit OAuth.

issues.title: Security considerations for revealing sensitive data in authorization details
issues.author: yaron-zehavi
issues.body:
Resource server providing actionable authorization details should avoid revealing sensitive data.
This falls also in the responsibility of the authorization server's design of authorization details types.

Generally:

- RAR Authoization details represent **request** semantics
- Resulting authorization_details granted to access tokens represent **approval** semantics and may contain richer content
- Request RAR objects should:
  - Avoid including end-user's sensitive data, instead having end-user provide required sensitive data by interacting with the authorization server.
  - Or use reference indicators (e.g use account #2 as reference instead of providing full account details)

See also:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9396.html#name-security-considerations

issues.title: Provide stable reference as authorization_hint guiding client on token selection
issues.author: yaron-zehavi

issues.title: Provide single or multi use semantics when providing remediation challenge
issues.author: yaron-zehavi

issues.title: Consider splitting into 2 drafts
issues.author: yaron-zehavi
issues.body:
1. OAuth 2.0 RAR Metadata Discovery. Defines how clients discover static RAR types metadata, including schemas, documentation, and examples, via dedicated endpoint.
2. OAuth 2.0 insufficient authorization failure signaling and remediation. Defines runtime failure signaling from the resource server when access tokens lack sufficient authorization details, including:

-   New normative error code in the WWW-Authenticate header.
-   Dynamic client guidance on required authorization details types returned in error responses.
-   Optional actionable authorization details objects based on the specific failing request input.
-   Optional guidance for clients on selecting existing eligible tokens.
-   Authorization server guidance on JWT access token issuance and introspection related to authorization_details claim size, sensitivity, and privacy considerations.

issues.title: Rar metadata returned in main AS metadata document or separate endpoint?
issues.author: yaron-zehavi
issues.body:
Pro separate endpoint:

- Don't overload main AS metadata document for verbose, large and sometimes esoteric topic
- Possible added latency for clients not performing http caching properly
- Readibility impact for people who read AS metadata

Con separate endpoint:

- Additional retrieval step

issues.title: Resource server should not respond using body, use www-authenticate only
issues.author: yaron-zehavi
issues.body: consider by reference when size of response element exceeds threshold

issues.title: Broad considerations for omitting RAR from JWT tokens
issues.author: yaron-zehavi
issues.body: It is RECOMMENDED that when an authorization server issues JWT access tokens, it should consider the size, sensitivity, and privacy implications of the authorization_details claim. Where appropriate, the authorization server SHOULD omit this claim from the JWT and instead make the approved RAR objects available to resource servers via the token introspection endpoint. This endpoint SHOULD require appropriate client authentication to prevent unauthorized access in case of token leakage.

issues.title: Clarify interoperability with any OAuth rfc and any grant that supports RAR
issues.author: yaron-zehavi

issues.title: Consider removing authorization_details_types_required
issues.author: yaron-zehavi
issues.body:
- RS optionally returns actionable RAR objects.
- Client checks AS metadata to verify support; if unsupported, attempts grant and handles errors.
- Client consults resource metadata to discover other supported ASs and attempts remediation accordingly.

This avoids Boolean logic expressions as provided by authorization_details_types_required.
Also simplifies RS concerns not having to figure out which AS can provide remediation, leaving it to the client.

pulls.title: Adds some norwegian examples and a mechanism for the RS to signal with types of authorization details it needs
pulls.author: runegri
pulls.body:
Adds a few json schema examples from the Norwegian Health Sector (HelseID). The structures are documented here https://utviklerportal.nhn.no/informasjonstjenester/helseid/bruksmoenstre-og-eksempelkode/bruk-av-helseid/docs/tillitsrammeverk/profil_for_tillitsrammeverkmd and here https://utviklerportal.nhn.no/informasjonstjenester/helseid/bruksmoenstre-og-eksempelkode/bruk-av-helseid/docs/tekniske-mekanismer/organisasjonsnumre_enmd. 

This PR also adds an alternative signaling mechanism that allows the RS to indicate which types of autorization details objects it requires without specifying the actual contents.

comments.author: runegri
comments.body:
Yes I my head the metadata and the required authorization details types may not match 1 to 1. For example in the HelseID we support several types of authorization details, but most APIs only require one of them. So while the `authorization_details_types_supported` endpoint will list several types, the response from the RS may be only one or two of the types. 

So I'm suggesting we open up the signaling mechanism a bit so that it is more generic. My impression is that in your ecosystem each API will require that the client supplies all supported authorization details types in the request?

comments.author: runegri
comments.body: Oh, and please do a read through of the examples. I have probably messed up the formatting here and there :)

reviews.author: yaron-zehavi
reviews.body:
Thanks @runegri this is highly appreciated!
About the error signaling + `authorization_details_required`, have you seen I've added to the resource metadata response (https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-zehavi-oauth-rar-metadata-01.html#name-oauth-20-protected-resource) the attribute `authorization_details_types_supported`, which is equivalent to your proposed error signaling?

If you did, what is the benefit you see in signaling directly via the error response?
Is it to skip a required HTTP call to resource metadata? Or to focus the client exactly on the required type?
